subject: ENGLISH
title: Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow
objective: Use simple regular verbs for past, present, and future time.
introduction: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb. You will explore examples, sort cards, practice, and answer five questions.

vocabulary.definition: A key word used in the lesson “Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow”.
A key word used in the lesson “Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow”.
A key word used in the lesson “Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow”.

activities.instruction: Study the idea and listen to the narration.
activities.content: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: Text alternative: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

activities.instruction: Explore each learning token, then read the examples.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: A numbered list of the same examples is available for keyboard and screen-reader use.

activities.instruction: Sort each card into “fits the lesson” or “does not fit.”

feedback.correct: Your groups follow the lesson rule.
feedback.retry: Use the explanation, not color or position.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Tap an item and then tap a labeled category; dragging is optional.

activities.instruction: Which sentence is about the future?

feedback.retry: Read the lesson rule again. We visited yesterday. and We visit every Monday. do not fit.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: All choices are visible text and selectable with TalkBack.

activities.instruction: Match each example to the lesson idea.

feedback.retry: Return to the explanation and compare one pair at a time.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: A list-based matching control replaces drag gestures.

activities.instruction: Complete the guided review in order.

feedback.correct: You are ready for the five-question check.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Up and down buttons provide an alternative to dragging.

items.prompt: Which statement best matches “Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow”?

options.text: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

items.explanation: The correct response is “Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

The correct response is “Yesterday we walked.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

items.prompt: Which choice does not follow the lesson idea?
items.explanation: The correct response is “Yesterday we will walk.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

items.prompt: Which sentence is about the future?
items.explanation: The correct response is “We will visit tomorrow.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

items.prompt: Which example should be used in the final review?
items.explanation: The correct response is “Tomorrow we will walk.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Regular past verbs often use -ed; present forms depend on the subject; future can use will plus the base verb.

assetSpecs.prompt: Friendly Grade 3 cartoon storybook concept board for Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow; rounded shapes, warm dark-brown outlines, tropical palette, no embedded text, uncluttered small-screen layout. Compose overlays provide labels and interactive targets.
assetSpecs.contentDescription: Five connected learning tokens for exploring examples in Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow.
